frontText: Create a namespace called `mynamespace`
backText:
```bash
kubectl create namespace mynamespace
```

Creates a new namespace imperatively. Namespaces isolate groups of resources within a single cluster. Short alias: `kubectl create ns mynamespace`.

[Namespaces](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/namespaces/)

frontText: Create a pod named `nginx` with image `nginx` in namespace `mynamespace`
backText:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never -n mynamespace
```

`kubectl run` creates a single pod. `--restart=Never` ensures a plain Pod is created (not a Deployment). `-n` selects the namespace.

[kubectl run reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#run)

frontText: Generate the YAML for a pod (image `nginx`, name `nginx`) **without creating it**, and save it to `pod.yaml`
backText:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
```

`--dry-run=client -o yaml` prints the manifest without submitting it to the cluster — the standard way to scaffold YAML in the exam. To include a command in the generated spec, append `--command -- env`. You can also pipe straight to creation: `... -o yaml | kubectl create -n mynamespace -f -`.

[kubectl conventions (--dry-run)](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/conventions/)

frontText: Create a resource from a YAML manifest file `pod.yaml`
backText:
```bash
kubectl create -f pod.yaml
```

Submits the manifest to the cluster. `kubectl apply -f pod.yaml` also works and is idempotent (creates or updates).

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

frontText: Create a `busybox` pod that runs the command `env`
backText:
```bash
kubectl run busybox --image=busybox --command --restart=Never -- env
```

`--command` makes everything after `--` the container's *command* (entrypoint) instead of its *args*. Add `-it --rm` to see the output directly and delete the pod afterwards, or check the output with `kubectl logs busybox`.

[Define a command and arguments for a container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-command-argument-container/)

frontText: Get the YAML for a new namespace called `myns` **without creating it**
backText:
```bash
kubectl create namespace myns -o yaml --dry-run=client
```

Same `--dry-run=client -o yaml` pattern as with pods — works for almost every `kubectl create` sub-command.

[Namespaces](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/namespaces/)

frontText: Create the YAML for a ResourceQuota called `myrq` with hard limits of 1 CPU, 1G memory and 2 pods, **without creating it**
backText:
```bash
kubectl create quota myrq --hard=cpu=1,memory=1G,pods=2 --dry-run=client -o yaml
```

`kubectl create quota` generates a ResourceQuota; `--hard` takes a comma-separated list of resource=quantity pairs.

[Resource Quotas](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/resource-quotas/)

frontText: Get pods in **all** namespaces
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po -A
```

`-A` is the short form of `--all-namespaces`. `po` is the short name for `pods`.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

frontText: Create a pod with image `nginx` called `nginx`, exposing (declaring) container port 80
backText:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --port=80
```

`--port` sets `containerPort: 80` in the pod spec. It documents the port the container listens on; it does not create a Service.

[kubectl run reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#run)

frontText: Change a running pod's image to `nginx:1.24.0`
backText:
```bash
kubectl set image pod/nginx nginx=nginx:1.24.0
```

Syntax: `kubectl set image POD/POD_NAME CONTAINER_NAME=IMAGE:TAG`. The container is killed and recreated with the new image as soon as it is pulled (the pod itself stays, its RESTARTS counter increments).

[Images](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/images/)

frontText: Watch a pod's status changes live
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po nginx -w
```

`-w` / `--watch` keeps the command running and prints a new line on every state change — useful to observe restarts and rollouts.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

frontText: Extract a single field from a pod with JSONPath (e.g. its image, or its IP)
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po nginx -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[].image}{"\n"}'
```

`-o jsonpath` extracts specific fields from an object. Another common use — get the pod IP:

```bash
kubectl get pod nginx -o jsonpath='{.status.podIP}'
```

[JSONPath support](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/jsonpath/)

frontText: List pods showing their IPs and nodes
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po -o wide
```

`-o wide` adds extra columns: pod IP, node, nominated node, readiness gates.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

frontText: Get information about a pod, including details about potential issues (e.g. pod hasn't started)
backText:
```bash
kubectl describe po nginx
```

`describe` shows the pod's configuration, conditions and — most importantly — the **Events** section at the bottom, which reveals scheduling/pull/start problems.

[Debug Pods](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/debug-pods/)

frontText: Get a running pod's YAML
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po nginx -o yaml
```

Prints the full live object, including `status`. Equivalent forms: `-oyaml`, `--output yaml`, `--output=yaml`.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

frontText: Get a pod's logs
backText:
```bash
kubectl logs nginx
```

Prints stdout/stderr of the pod's (single) container. Add `-f` to follow, `-c <container>` for multi-container pods.

[Logging Architecture](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/logging/)

frontText: A pod crashed and restarted — get the logs of the **previous** container instance
backText:
```bash
kubectl logs nginx -p
```

`-p` / `--previous` shows logs of the prior, terminated instance of the container — essential when debugging crash loops.

[Debug Running Pods](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/debug-running-pod/)

frontText: Execute an interactive shell on the `nginx` pod
backText:
```bash
kubectl exec -it nginx -- /bin/sh
```

`-it` attaches an interactive terminal; everything after `--` is the command run inside the container.

[Get a Shell to a Running Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/get-shell-running-container/)

frontText: Run a **temporary** pod (interactive, deleted automatically when it exits) — e.g. a busybox to `wget` another pod's IP
backText:
```bash
kubectl run busybox --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never -- wget -O- 10.1.1.131:80
```

`--rm` deletes the pod right after it exits; `-it` shows the output interactively. Ideal for one-off connectivity checks and throwaway commands, e.g. `-- /bin/sh -c 'echo hello world'`.

[kubectl run reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#run)

frontText: Create a busybox pod that echoes 'hello world' and then exits, showing the output
backText:
```bash
kubectl run busybox --image=busybox -it --restart=Never -- echo 'hello world'
```

Everything after `--` (without `--command`) becomes the container's *args*, passed to the image's default entrypoint. Also valid: `-- /bin/sh -c 'echo hello world'`.

[Define a command and arguments for a container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-command-argument-container/)

frontText: Create an `nginx` pod with the environment variable `var1=val1`
backText:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --env=var1=val1
```

`--env=KEY=VALUE` can be repeated for multiple variables. Verify with `kubectl exec -it nginx -- sh -c 'echo $var1'` or `kubectl describe po nginx | grep val1`.

[Define Environment Variables for a Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-environment-variable-container/)

frontText: Print all environment variables inside a running pod
backText:
```bash
kubectl exec -it nginx -- env
```

Runs `env` inside the container. Handy to verify injected variables from `--env`, ConfigMaps or Secrets.

[Get a Shell to a Running Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/get-shell-running-container/)

frontText: Define a pod with **two** busybox containers, both running `echo hello; sleep 3600` (edit generated YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod.yaml
```

Generate a single-container pod with `kubectl run ... --dry-run=client -o yaml`, then duplicate the entry under `spec.containers` and give it a **different name**:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'echo hello;sleep 3600']
    image: busybox
    name: busybox
  - args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'echo hello;sleep 3600']
    image: busybox
    name: busybox2
```

There is no imperative flag for multiple containers — you always edit the YAML.

[Pods with multiple containers](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/#how-pods-manage-multiple-containers)

frontText: Run a command (e.g. `ls`) in a **specific container** of a multi-container pod (pod `busybox`, container `busybox2`)
backText:
```bash
kubectl exec -it busybox -c busybox2 -- ls
```

`-c` / `--container` selects the container; without it kubectl uses the first container (or the one annotated as default). Same flag works for an interactive shell (`-- /bin/sh`) and for `kubectl logs`.

[Get a Shell to a Running Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/get-shell-running-container/)

frontText: Share an `emptyDir` volume between containers of a pod — define the volume and mount it (e.g. at `/usr/share/nginx/html`) (edit generated YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod-init.yaml
```

Declare the volume at `spec.volumes` and mount it via `volumeMounts` in every container that needs it (each container can use a different `mountPath`):

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - image: nginx
    name: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: vol
      mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumes:
  - name: vol
    emptyDir: {}
```

`emptyDir` starts empty, lives as long as the pod, and is the standard way to share files between containers of the same pod.

[emptyDir volumes](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes/#emptydir)

frontText: Add a busybox **init container** that runs `echo "Test" > /work-dir/index.html` with a volume mounted at `/work-dir` (edit generated YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod-init.yaml
```

Init containers go under `spec.initContainers` (same schema as regular containers) and run to completion, in order, **before** the app containers start:

```yaml
spec:
  initContainers:
  - args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'echo "Test" > /work-dir/index.html']
    image: busybox
    name: box
    volumeMounts:
    - name: vol
      mountPath: /work-dir
```

Combined with a shared `emptyDir` volume, this is the classic pattern for pre-populating content (e.g. an nginx web root).

[Init Containers](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/init-containers/)

frontText: Create 3 pods `nginx1`, `nginx2`, `nginx3`, all with the label `app=v1`
backText:
```bash
kubectl run nginx1 --image=nginx --restart=Never --labels=app=v1
```

`--labels` (short form `-l`) sets labels at creation time. Repeat for each pod, or loop:

```bash
for i in $(seq 1 3); do kubectl run nginx$i --image=nginx -l app=v1; done
```

[Labels and Selectors](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/)

frontText: Show all pods **with their labels**
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po --show-labels
```

Adds a LABELS column with every label of every pod — the quickest way to verify labeling operations.

[Labels and Selectors](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/)

frontText: Change the label of pod `nginx2` to `app=v2`
backText:
```bash
kubectl label po nginx2 app=v2 --overwrite
```

Without `--overwrite` kubectl refuses to modify an existing label. Alternative: `kubectl edit po nginx2` and change it in the YAML.

[kubectl label reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#label)

frontText: List pods with a dedicated **column** showing the value of the `app` label
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po -L app
```

`-L` / `--label-columns` adds one column per listed label key (comma-separated for several).

[Labels and Selectors](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/)

frontText: Get only the pods with label `app=v2`
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po -l app=v2
```

`-l` / `--selector` filters by label. Equivalent forms: `--selector=app=v2`, set-based `-l 'app in (v2)'`. Works for any resource type, e.g. `kubectl get rs -l app=nginx`.

[Label selectors](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/#label-selectors)

frontText: Get pods with `app=v2` but **not** `tier=frontend`
backText:
```bash
kubectl get po -l app=v2,tier!=frontend
```

Comma means AND; `!=` negates. Set-based equivalent: `-l 'app in (v2), tier notin (frontend)'`.

[Label selectors](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/#label-selectors)

frontText: Add a new label `tier=web` to **all** pods having label `app=v1` or `app=v2`
backText:
```bash
kubectl label po -l "app in(v1,v2)" tier=web
```

Select the target pods with `-l` (set-based selector), then state the label to add — label many objects with one command.

[kubectl label reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#label)

frontText: Annotate pods `nginx1`, `nginx2`, `nginx3` with `description='my description'` (or all `app=v2` pods with `owner=marketing`)
backText:
```bash
kubectl annotate po nginx1 nginx2 nginx3 description='my description'
```

Shell brace expansion also works: `kubectl annotate po nginx{1..3} ...`. By selector instead of names: `kubectl annotate po -l "app=v2" owner=marketing`. Annotations hold non-identifying metadata and cannot be used in selectors.

[Annotations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/annotations/)

frontText: **Remove** the `app` label from pods `nginx1`, `nginx2`, `nginx3`
backText:
```bash
kubectl label po nginx{1..3} app-
```

A trailing `-` after the key removes the label. By selector: `kubectl label po -l app app-` (all pods that have an `app` label).

[kubectl label reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#label)

frontText: Check the annotations of pod `nginx1`
backText:
```bash
kubectl annotate pod nginx1 --list
```

Alternatives: `kubectl describe po nginx1 | grep -i annotations` or `kubectl get po nginx1 -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}{"\n"}'`.

[Annotations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/annotations/)

frontText: **Remove** the annotations `description` and `owner` from pods `nginx1`, `nginx2`, `nginx3`
backText:
```bash
kubectl annotate po nginx{1..3} description- owner-
```

Same trailing-`-` convention as removing labels; several keys can be removed in one command.

[Annotations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/annotations/)

frontText: Delete several resources with a single command (e.g. pods `nginx1..3`, or a deployment **and** its HPA)
backText:
```bash
kubectl delete po nginx{1..3}
```

List several names of one type, or mix types with the `type/name` form:

```bash
kubectl delete deploy/nginx hpa/nginx
```

The same pattern deletes jobs (`kubectl delete job pi`) and cron jobs (`kubectl delete cj busybox`).

[kubectl delete reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#delete)

frontText: Label a **node** with `accelerator=nvidia-tesla-p100`
backText:
```bash
kubectl label nodes <node-name> accelerator=nvidia-tesla-p100
```

`kubectl label` works on any resource, nodes included. Verify with `kubectl get nodes --show-labels`.

[Assign Pods to Nodes](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/assign-pods-nodes/)

frontText: Schedule a pod only on nodes with label `accelerator=nvidia-tesla-p100` using `nodeSelector` (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod.yaml
```

`nodeSelector` is the simplest node-selection constraint — a map of labels the node must have:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: cuda-test
    image: "k8s.gcr.io/cuda-vector-add:v0.1"
  nodeSelector:
    accelerator: nvidia-tesla-p100
```

For `kubernetes.io/hostname: <node>` it pins the pod to a specific node (combine with tolerations for control-plane nodes).

[nodeSelector](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/assign-pod-node/#nodeselector)

frontText: Schedule a pod using **node affinity** (required rule matching label `accelerator=nvidia-tesla-p100`) (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod.yaml
```

Node affinity is the more expressive alternative to `nodeSelector`:

```yaml
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: accelerator
            operator: In
            values:
            - nvidia-tesla-p100
```

`required...` is a hard rule; `preferred...` is a soft preference.

[Node affinity](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/assign-pod-node/#node-affinity)

frontText: Find where a field belongs in a resource's YAML (e.g. where does `nodeSelector` go in a pod spec?)
backText:
```bash
kubectl explain po.spec
```

`kubectl explain` prints the built-in API schema documentation — no internet needed in the exam. Drill down with dotted paths (`kubectl explain po.spec.affinity`) and add `--recursive` to see the whole subtree.

[kubectl explain](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/generated/kubectl_explain/)

frontText: Force a pod onto node `node01`, bypassing the scheduler (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod.yaml
```

`nodeName` binds the pod directly to the named node — no scheduling involved:

```yaml
spec:
  nodeName: node01
  containers:
  - name: nodename-con
    image: nginx
```

Verify where it landed: `kubectl get pod nodename-pod -o wide`.

[nodeName](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/assign-pod-node/#nodename)

frontText: Taint a node with key `tier`, value `frontend` and effect `NoSchedule`
backText:
```bash
kubectl taint node node1 tier=frontend:NoSchedule
```

Syntax is `key=value:Effect` (effects: `NoSchedule`, `PreferNoSchedule`, `NoExecute`). View taints with `kubectl describe node node1`; remove by appending `-`: `kubectl taint node node1 tier=frontend:NoSchedule-`.

[Taints and Tolerations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/taint-and-toleration/)

frontText: Create a pod that **tolerates** the taint `tier=frontend:NoSchedule` (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi pod.yaml
```

Tolerations go under `spec.tolerations`:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
  tolerations:
  - key: "tier"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "frontend"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
```

Use `operator: "Exists"` (no value) to tolerate any value of a key — e.g. `key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane` to allow scheduling on control-plane nodes. A toleration only *permits* scheduling; combine with `nodeSelector` to *force* it.

[Taints and Tolerations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/taint-and-toleration/)

frontText: Create a deployment `nginx` with image `nginx:1.18.0`, 2 replicas, container port 80 (no service)
backText:
```bash
kubectl create deploy nginx --image=nginx:1.18.0 --replicas=2 --port=80
```

All three flags are supported imperatively. Alternatively scaffold with `kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx:1.18.0 --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml`, edit `replicas` and add `ports: [containerPort: 80]`, then `kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml`.

[Deployments](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/)

frontText: View the YAML of deployment `nginx`
backText:
```bash
kubectl get deploy nginx -o yaml
```

Same `-o yaml` pattern as for pods — works on every resource type (`deploy` is the short name for `deployments`).

[Deployments](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/)

frontText: Find the ReplicaSet created by deployment `nginx` (and view its YAML)
backText:
```bash
kubectl get rs -l app=nginx
```

`kubectl create deploy` labels everything with `app=<name>`. Then `kubectl get rs <rs-name> -o yaml` shows the YAML. You can also spot the ReplicaSet in `kubectl describe deploy nginx` (the `NewReplicaSet` field and Events), or just run `kubectl get rs`.

[ReplicaSet](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/replicaset/)

frontText: Check how the rollout of deployment `nginx` is going
backText:
```bash
kubectl rollout status deploy nginx
```

Blocks until the rollout succeeds or reports what it is waiting for. If it hangs, `kubectl get po` typically reveals the cause (e.g. `ErrImagePull` / `ImagePullBackOff` after a bad image update).

[Deployment rollout status](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#checking-rollout-status)

frontText: Update the image of deployment `nginx` to `nginx:1.19.8`
backText:
```bash
kubectl set image deploy nginx nginx=nginx:1.19.8
```

Syntax: `kubectl set image TYPE NAME CONTAINER_NAME=IMAGE:TAG`. On a deployment this triggers a rolling update and creates a new revision. Alternative: `kubectl edit deploy nginx` and change `.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image`.

[Updating a Deployment](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#updating-a-deployment)

frontText: Check the rollout **history** of deployment `nginx` (and the details of revision 4)
backText:
```bash
kubectl rollout history deploy nginx
```

Lists all revisions. Details of a single revision (image used, etc.):

```bash
kubectl rollout history deploy nginx --revision=4
```

[Checking rollout history](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#checking-rollout-history-of-a-deployment)

frontText: Undo the latest rollout of deployment `nginx` (or return to a **specific** revision)
backText:
```bash
kubectl rollout undo deploy nginx
```

Rolls back to the previous revision. To go to a specific one:

```bash
kubectl rollout undo deploy nginx --to-revision=2
```

Verify with `kubectl describe deploy nginx | grep Image:` and `kubectl rollout status deploy nginx`.

[Rolling back a Deployment](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#rolling-back-a-deployment)

frontText: Scale deployment `nginx` to 5 replicas
backText:
```bash
kubectl scale deploy nginx --replicas=5
```

Works on deployments, replica sets and stateful sets. Verify with `kubectl get po` and `kubectl describe deploy nginx`.

[Scaling a Deployment](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#scaling-a-deployment)

frontText: Autoscale deployment `nginx` between 5 and 10 pods, targeting 80% CPU utilization
backText:
```bash
kubectl autoscale deploy nginx --min=5 --max=10 --cpu-percent=80
```

Creates a HorizontalPodAutoscaler. Inspect it with `kubectl get hpa nginx`. Delete it together with the deployment when cleaning up (`kubectl delete deploy/nginx hpa/nginx`).

[Horizontal Pod Autoscaling](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-autoscale/)

frontText: **Pause** the rollout of deployment `nginx`
backText:
```bash
kubectl rollout pause deploy nginx
```

While paused, spec changes (e.g. `kubectl set image ...`) are recorded but do **not** trigger a rollout or a new revision — `kubectl rollout history` stays unchanged until you resume.

[Pausing and Resuming a rollout](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#pausing-and-resuming-a-deployment)

frontText: **Resume** the paused rollout of deployment `nginx`
backText:
```bash
kubectl rollout resume deploy nginx
```

All changes accumulated during the pause are rolled out as a single new revision — confirm with `kubectl rollout history deploy nginx` (and `--revision=<latest>`).

[Pausing and Resuming a rollout](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#pausing-and-resuming-a-deployment)

frontText: Implement a **canary deployment**: nginx v1 and v2 behind one service, load balanced 75%–25% (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi canary.yaml
```

Two deployments whose pod templates share a common label (`app: my-app`) but differ in a `version` label; the Service selects **only** the shared label, so it balances across both. The traffic ratio comes from the replica counts (3 × v1, 1 × v2 → 75%–25%):

```yaml
# deployment my-app-v1: replicas: 3, template labels: {app: my-app, version: v1}
# deployment my-app-v2: replicas: 1, template labels: {app: my-app, version: v2}
---
kind: Service
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app   # no version -> hits both deployments
```

Promote by scaling v2 up (`kubectl scale --replicas=4 deploy my-app-v2`) and deleting v1.

[Canary deployments](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/manage-deployment/#canary-deployments)

frontText: Create a job named `pi` with image `perl:5.34` that runs `perl -Mbignum=bpi -wle 'print bpi(2000)'`
backText:
```bash
kubectl create job pi --image=perl:5.34 -- perl -Mbignum=bpi -wle 'print bpi(2000)'
```

Everything after `--` is the container command. Same pattern with a shell: `kubectl create job busybox --image=busybox -- /bin/sh -c 'echo hello;sleep 30;echo world'`. Add `--dry-run=client -o yaml > job.yaml` to scaffold the YAML for editing.

[Jobs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/)

frontText: Watch a job until it completes (or block until it is done)
backText:
```bash
kubectl get jobs -w
```

`-w` watches the COMPLETIONS column live. To block in a script until the job finishes:

```bash
kubectl wait --for=condition=complete --timeout=300s job pi
```

[Jobs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/)

frontText: Get the logs of a job **without looking up its pod name**
backText:
```bash
kubectl logs job/pi
```

`kubectl logs` accepts `job/<name>` and resolves the job's pod for you — faster than `kubectl get po` + `kubectl logs <pod>`.

[Jobs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/)

frontText: Make a job run **5 times, one after the other** (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi job.yaml
```

Set `completions` on the job spec (optionally with Indexed mode):

```yaml
spec:
  completions: 5
  completionMode: Indexed
  template:
    ...
```

The job runs pods sequentially until 5 of them succeed. Watch with `kubectl get job busybox -w`.

[Job completions](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/#parallel-jobs)

frontText: Make a job run **5 pods in parallel** (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi job.yaml
```

Set `parallelism` on the job spec:

```yaml
spec:
  parallelism: 5
  template:
    ...
```

Five pods start at once. Combine with `completions` for "N successes, M at a time".

[Parallel Jobs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/#parallel-jobs)

frontText: Terminate a job automatically if it runs for more than 30 seconds (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi job.yaml
```

Set `activeDeadlineSeconds` on the **job** spec (not the pod spec):

```yaml
spec:
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 30
  template:
    ...
```

Kubernetes kills the job's pods and marks the job `DeadlineExceeded` once the deadline passes. For jobs created by a CronJob, put it at `cronjob.spec.jobTemplate.spec.activeDeadlineSeconds`.

[Job termination and cleanup](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/#job-termination-and-cleanup)

frontText: Create a cron job `busybox` that runs every minute and prints the date plus 'Hello from the Kubernetes cluster'
backText:
```bash
kubectl create cronjob busybox --image=busybox --schedule="*/1 * * * *" -- /bin/sh -c 'date; echo Hello from the Kubernetes cluster'
```

`--schedule` takes standard cron syntax. Add `--dry-run=client -o yaml > cj.yaml` to scaffold the YAML for editing (e.g. to add deadlines).

[Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/job/automated-tasks-with-cron-jobs/)

frontText: List cron jobs
backText:
```bash
kubectl get cj
```

`cj` is the short name for `cronjob`. Each firing creates a **Job**, which creates a **Pod** — watch them appear with `kubectl get jobs --watch`, and note that the pods carry a label naming their parent job (`kubectl get po --show-labels`).

[CronJob](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/cron-job/)

frontText: Make a cron job's run be skipped/terminated if it misses its scheduled start by more than 17 seconds (edit YAML)
backText:
```bash
vi time-limited-job.yaml
```

Set `startingDeadlineSeconds` on the **cronjob** spec:

```yaml
spec:
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 17
  schedule: '* * * * *'
  jobTemplate:
    ...
```

If the job cannot start within 17 s after its scheduled time, that run counts as missed.

[CronJob - starting deadline](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/cron-job/#starting-deadline)

frontText: Trigger a cron job **manually** — create a job from an existing cron job
backText:
```bash
kubectl create job --from=cronjob/sample-cron-job sample-job
```

`--from=cronjob/<name>` copies the cron job's job template into a one-off job that runs immediately.

[Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/job/automated-tasks-with-cron-jobs/)
